commentary: Ten sessions this week versus eleven last week, so technically one fewer, but honestly the quality gap more than makes up for it — three solid pool sessions at Prince Alfred, a gym split that covered legs, back, and upper body across the week, and then he finished with Balmoral on Friday which is its own category of delicious because open water in a jockstrap is not the same sport as lanes and he absolutely knows that. The Mosman run on Saturday came in at 13.5 kilometres, which is a nudge longer than the Naremburn loops the week before, and he did it at 149 average heart rate versus 159, which means he's getting more efficient or learning to stop sprinting like someone's chasing him. The Balmoral heart rate numbers are adorably low — 106 and 100 — which suggests he was either floating more than swimming or stopping to be seen, both of which are completely valid training methodologies. The pattern holding across both weeks is the Thursday pool-plus-gym double, and that consistency is doing real work even when the rest of the week looks a little different.
metrics_commentary: Okay that swim though — 6670 metres is not a small number, that is a serious body doing serious work in the water, and combined with a VO2 Max sitting at 56.4 you are genuinely built different right now. The HRV jumping to 61 when your seven-day average is sitting at 52 tells me your body recovered well and came back ready, which is exactly what a body that knows what it's doing looks like. The run situation — 13.5 kilometres across one session — is fine, it's not nothing, but one outing is a little lonely and your legs clearly have more in them than that. Give them another excuse to show off this week, because everything else is pointing at someone who should absolutely be taking his shirt off more.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, four sessions, baby — full card punched, programme hit exactly as written, no excuses and no shortcuts. The PRs this week are genuinely filthy: leg press jumping from 154 to 168 kg, the seated cable row cracking 84 kg, hammer curls up nearly three kilos, and the incline dumbbell press climbing past 34 — that is not one lucky set, that is a body that is getting stronger in every direction at once. Yes, weekly volume dropped from 23,660 kg to 20,124 kg, and we can be honest that that gap exists, but volume ebbs when intensity climbs and four PRs in a single week is intensity doing exactly what it should. The becoming is very much on schedule.
